comments.text: I’ve tried so many scalping strategies, but they never seem to work for me. I keep getting in too early or too late, and I always end up overtrading. Is this “stupid simple” strategy really worth it, or just another gimmick?

Which strategy has a higher win rate in your experience: This strategy you call the "One Candle Scalping Strategy or using the ORB and the "Touch and Turn Scalper?"  Thanking you in advance for your reply.  Good stuff, Carl.

Losing my mind with 1-minute candle setups. Is it even possible to survive this strategy? I’ve tried 5 different approaches, and every session ends the same either I get stopped out immediately or I panic-trade and blow my account. These quick candles move too fast and the videos make it look easy, but the reality is brutal. Is there anyone out there who actually teaches how to read this strategy in real time and protect your money? I need genuine guidance before I lose any more.

Interpreting candlestick patterns are quite subjective. How much of a wick actually makes a hammer? Does the engulfing candle actually have to "engulf" the previous one? - they didn't in the examples, but were identified as "engulfing" anyway. If you really want to see how this works in a mechanical and non-subjective way, you should program (and trade) it in backtesting software.  You have to find the one that makes sense to you, but my preference is WealthLab for that.

I am curious - do you have rules for negating the setup if the chart reverses immediately after the opening 15min, and doesn't continue outside the boxed area straight away - do you have a specific condition where the setup is thrown out.
For example, is there a percentage retracement of the liquidity candle that invalidates the setup.
Or, conversely, is there a persentage retracement of the liquidity candle that makes you think the reversal happened more quickly on a smaller time frame.

Just this morning (2nd July) - I was watching the AUS200 index and the reversal happened very quickly after the opening 15min candle, so nothing ever happened outside of the boxed area on the downside. I am assuming that this would just be a reversal that is missed because it happened too quickly and didn't meet the rules.

ORRRR --- do you (as an advanced trader and as an additional layer to this method) also consider the timeframe based on "how long it takes to move the required distance of 25% of average daily true range" and then monitor the entry on a revised time scale??
Or does that just make it overly complex and reduce probability of success....

the flaw in this strategy is that you only get 1 chance a day, because if the 90 minute window does not work out, you are done for the day, so it is not a very efficient use of time. there needs to be a way to identify the stocks that would have a good chance to succeed pre-market. Also, even if it works, the 90 minute window only gives you a few percentage move for most stocks, so it would be helpful to also point out about position size to make any meaningful gain.
